persons.name: Lawrence Summers
persons.role: Treasury Secretary (1999–2001); NEC Director (2009–2010); Harvard President (2001–2006)
persons.bluf: A central architect of late-1990s financial deregulation who later became the loudest establishment Cassandra on 2021 inflation. Assess with HIGH confidence his fingerprints on Gramm-Leach-Bliley (Glass-Steagall repeal) and the Commodity Futures Modernization Act (which exempted OTC derivatives), and MODERATE-HIGH that his 2021 'too much fiscal stimulus' warning was directionally correct against the Fed/Treasury consensus.
persons.drivers: Intellectual dominance; being proven right; proximity to power across administrations.
persons.worldview: Neoliberal-Keynesian hybrid: pro-market deregulation in the 1990s, but a believer in large counter-cyclical intervention and, latterly, in supply-constrained inflation risk and 'secular stagnation.'
persons.decision_style: Combative, supremely self-assured, willing to break with consensus publicly; prone to abrasive certainty.
persons.track_record: Backed OTC-derivatives exemption (CFMA 2000) and Glass-Steagall repeal (1999); opposed Brooksley Born's CFTC swaps-oversight push; warned in 2021 that ARP-scale stimulus would overheat the economy (vindicated); long associated with the 'activist Treasury issuance' critique he now partly voices.
persons.vulnerabilities: The Born clash and CFMA are cited in post-2008 and crypto-derivatives post-mortems; the Harvard presidency ended amid controversy; perceived as emblematic of the bipartisan dereg consensus he now critiques.
persons.relationships: Tied to Rubin (mentor) and Geithner (protégé); public sparring partner of the Yellen/Powell 'transitory' camp; informal advisor across Democratic administrations.
persons.outlook: His dereg legacy and his inflation warnings are both load-bearing in the macro narrative; a credibility weather-vane for elite consensus.
persons.confidence: HIGH (legislative record); MODERATE-HIGH (intent and influence interpreted).

persons.name: Wendy Gramm
persons.bluf: The CFTC chair whose final-days 1993 exemption for energy derivatives — issued days before she joined Enron's board — became the textbook case of the deregulation-to-revolving-door pipeline. Assess with HIGH confidence on the documented sequence; intent is interpreted.
persons.drivers: Free-market conviction; deregulatory mission; post-government board compensation.
persons.worldview: Austrian/Chicago-school deregulator; viewed most derivatives oversight as economically harmful friction.
persons.decision_style: Ideologically driven, willing to use rulemaking and exemptive authority aggressively to shrink CFTC reach.
persons.track_record: Granted the 1993 'Enron exemption' for OTC energy swaps; resigned the CFTC and joined Enron's board within weeks, serving on its audit committee until the 2001 collapse; later at the Mercatus Center.
persons.vulnerabilities: The exemption + immediate Enron board seat is the canonical revolving-door/regulatory-capture exhibit; the audit-committee role during the fraud is a permanent reputational anchor.
persons.relationships: Spouse of Senator Phil Gramm (co-author of the CFMA that codified the OTC-derivatives carve-out); part of the broader Gramm deregulatory legacy.
persons.outlook: A historical template for how exemptive authority + revolving door recur in today's crypto-derivatives and stablecoin debates.

persons.name: Lael Brainard
persons.role: Fed Governor (2014–2022); Fed Vice Chair (2022–2023); NEC Director (2023–2025)
persons.bluf: A dovish, regulation-forward Fed governor who became the leading internal advocate for a US central-bank digital currency and for tighter bank-climate and capital rules, then ran Biden's NEC. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence she was the Fed's principal CBDC and pro-regulation voice during the 2017–2022 dissents.
persons.drivers: Financial-stability priority; consumer protection; institutional reform.
persons.worldview: Interventionist Democrat; skeptical of light-touch supervision; favors public-option payment infrastructure (CBDC) over private stablecoins.
persons.decision_style: Detail-driven, willing to dissent solo against the Quarles deregulatory agenda; coalition-builder on the Board.
persons.track_record: Cast repeated dissents against post-2018 supervisory loosening; championed the Fed CBDC research; as NEC director helped shape Biden-era industrial/climate-finance policy.
persons.vulnerabilities: Her CBDC advocacy is a lightning rod in the stablecoin-vs-public-money debate; criticized by the right as politicizing supervision.
persons.relationships: Counterweight to Quarles/Bowman deregulators; aligned with Yellen on financial-stability framing.
persons.outlook: Her CBDC and supervision positions frame the live stablecoin/payments-rail contest.

persons.name: Randal Quarles
persons.role: Fed Vice Chair for Supervision (2017–2021); Fed Governor (2017–2021)
persons.bluf: The first-ever Fed Vice Chair for Supervision, whose 'tailoring' of post-crisis rules — raising the $50B SIFI threshold and easing liquidity/stress-test requirements for mid-size banks — is widely linked to the supervisory gaps exposed by the 2023 SVB/Signature failures. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence on the regulatory-loosening record; causal link to 2023 is contested.
persons.drivers: Deregulatory efficiency; reducing 'gold-plating' of bank rules; private-equity-aligned worldview.
persons.worldview: Market-friendly Republican; believed post-Dodd-Frank rules were excessive for non-G-SIB banks.
persons.decision_style: Methodical, lawyerly, incremental; implemented EGRRCPA's 2018 tailoring mandate enthusiastically.
persons.track_record: Led the 2018–2019 tailoring rules that relaxed standards for $100–250B banks (the SVB cohort); chaired the FSB; returned to private investing (Cynosure) afterward.
persons.vulnerabilities: His name is central to the post-SVB 'who weakened the rules' debate; Carlyle/private-equity ties feed the capture narrative.
persons.relationships: Implemented the Trump-era dereg agenda over Brainard's dissents; predecessor framing to Bowman's later supervision role.
persons.outlook: A direct line from his tailoring rules to the HTM/duration-risk and regional-bank-fragility threads.

persons.name: Daniel Tarullo
persons.role: Fed Governor (2009–2017); de facto head of bank supervision
persons.bluf: The unofficial architect of the post-2008 US bank-regulation regime — stress tests (CCAR), the G-SIB surcharge, and tougher capital — who ran Fed supervision before the role was formalized. Assess with HIGH confidence he built the framework that Quarles later loosened.
persons.drivers: Financial-stability primacy; macroprudential rigor; institutional memory of 2008.
persons.worldview: Hawkish on bank capital and systemic risk; skeptical of bank self-assessment of risk.
persons.decision_style: Intellectually forceful, technically deep, willing to impose binding constraints over industry objection.
persons.track_record: Designed and ran CCAR/DFAST stress testing; pushed the G-SIB capital surcharge and the supplementary leverage ratio; warned on exit about premature deregulation.
persons.vulnerabilities: Criticized by banks as heavy-handed; his framework's later dilution is the counter-case in the dereg debate.
persons.relationships: Predecessor/foil to Quarles and Bowman; aligned with Brainard on tough supervision.
persons.outlook: His framework is the baseline against which every subsequent loosening (and every bank failure) is measured.

persons.name: Richard Clarida
persons.bluf: Powell's vice chair and the intellectual author of the 2020 'flexible average inflation targeting' (FAIT) framework — the doctrine later blamed for the Fed's delayed 2021–2022 inflation response — whose tenure ended early amid scrutiny of personal trades during the 2020 market turmoil. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence FAIT contributed to the late liftoff.
persons.drivers: Academic legacy (the FAIT framework); reputation; market credibility.
persons.worldview: New-Keynesian; believed in pre-committing to inflation overshoots to anchor expectations near zero rates.
persons.decision_style: Cerebral, framework-driven, consensus-aligned with Powell.
persons.track_record: Co-authored the 2020 strategy review and FAIT; resigned weeks early in 2022 after disclosure of well-timed 2020 fund transactions (part of the broader Fed trading scandal).
persons.vulnerabilities: FAIT is central to the 'Fed was late' critique; the trading-disclosure episode dented the Fed's ethics standing alongside the Kaplan/Rosengren resignations.
persons.relationships: Powell ally; part of the cohort whose 2020–2021 trades forced new Fed ethics rules.
persons.outlook: FAIT's design choices are a root cause in the inflation-timing narrative.

persons.name: Stanley Fischer
persons.role: Fed Vice Chair (2014–2017); Bank of Israel Governor (2005–2013); IMF First Deputy MD (1994–2001)
persons.bluf: A towering academic central banker — MIT mentor to Bernanke and Draghi — who bridged the IMF, Bank of Israel, Citigroup, and the Fed, embodying the global monetary-policy establishment. Assess with HIGH confidence on his outsized influence over a generation of central bankers.
persons.drivers: Intellectual stewardship of the global monetary order; crisis management.
persons.worldview: Pragmatic Keynesian-monetarist synthesis; lender-of-last-resort interventionism.
persons.decision_style: Calm, authoritative, internationally networked; comfortable with unconventional policy.
persons.track_record: Steered IMF crisis programs (1990s EM crises), ran the Bank of Israel through 2008 with early easing, served as Citigroup vice chairman, then Fed vice chair under Yellen.
persons.vulnerabilities: The Citigroup interlude feeds the revolving-door critique; IMF-era austerity programs remain contested.
persons.relationships: Mentor to Bernanke, Draghi, and much of the modern central-banking elite; Yellen-era partner.
persons.outlook: A node connecting the IMF, private banking, and the Fed in the macro-establishment map.

persons.name: Kevin Warsh
persons.bluf: The youngest-ever Fed governor, a crisis-era market liaison turned persistent QE critic and perennial Fed-chair contender. Assess with MODERATE confidence he represents the hawkish, market-structure-focused wing repeatedly considered to lead the Fed.
persons.drivers: Influence over monetary direction; market credibility; positioning for the chairmanship.
persons.worldview: Hawkish; skeptical of prolonged balance-sheet expansion and of the Fed's market interventions; pro-normalization.
persons.decision_style: Market-savvy, politically attuned, media-fluent; an internal/external bridge during 2008.
persons.track_record: Served as the Fed's Wall Street liaison through the 2008 crisis; dissented toward tighter policy; left in 2011 and became a vocal QE critic; recurrently floated as a chair pick.
persons.vulnerabilities: Critics note Morgan Stanley/PE ties and inconsistency between crisis-era activism and later hawkishness.
persons.relationships: Bernanke-era colleague; connected to Republican economic circles and Stanford's Hoover network.
persons.outlook: A live candidate-in-waiting whose elevation would shift the Fed hawkish — a scenario variable.

persons.name: John C. Williams
persons.role: President, Federal Reserve Bank of New York (2018–present); permanent FOMC Vice Chair
persons.bluf: The NY Fed president — permanent FOMC vice chair and manager of the System Open Market Account — and co-author of the influential 'r-star' (neutral-rate) estimates that anchor the Fed's policy framing. Assess with HIGH confidence on his structural centrality to FOMC execution.
persons.drivers: Operational stewardship of monetary policy; analytical rigor on the neutral rate.
persons.worldview: Mainstream New-Keynesian; data-dependent; emphasizes the low long-run neutral rate.
persons.decision_style: Technocratic, low-drama, execution-focused; the FOMC's operational center of gravity.
persons.track_record: Ran the SF Fed research shop (r-star work with Holston-Laubach-Williams), then NY Fed; manages the desk that implements every FOMC decision and the repo facilities.
persons.vulnerabilities: His r-star estimates shape how 'restrictive' policy is judged — a contested analytical input; less a personal vulnerability than a framework dependency.
persons.relationships: Powell's operational right hand; permanent voter regardless of rotation.
persons.outlook: Controls policy implementation and the repo plumbing — a structural rather than rotating influence node.

persons.name: Christopher Waller
persons.bluf: A Fed governor who became the Board's most articulate stablecoin proponent and CBDC skeptic — arguing private dollar-stablecoins extend dollar dominance and that a US CBDC is a solution in search of a problem. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence he anchors the pro-private-stablecoin, anti-CBDC pole inside the Fed.
persons.drivers: Monetary-economics rigor; preserving dollar primacy via private rails; opposing payment-system state expansion.
persons.worldview: Market-oriented; sees well-regulated stablecoins as complementary to, not threatening, the dollar system.
persons.decision_style: Academic, plain-spoken, willing to stake out clear positions on crypto and rates.
persons.track_record: Published prominent speeches favoring stablecoins and questioning a CBDC; a relatively hawkish-to-neutral rates voice; frequently cited in the GENIUS Act / stablecoin-regulation debate.
persons.vulnerabilities: His pro-stablecoin stance aligns him (in critics' view) with industry interests amid the debanking and reserve-quality debates.
persons.relationships: Counterpole to Brainard's CBDC advocacy; aligned with the Trump-era pro-stablecoin policy tilt.
persons.outlook: A key Fed voice shaping whether stablecoins or a CBDC win the dollar-rail contest.

persons.name: Michelle Bowman
persons.role: Fed Vice Chair for Supervision (2025–present); Fed Governor (2018–present)
persons.bluf: The community-banker-turned-Fed-governor elevated in 2025 to Vice Chair for Supervision, signaling a deregulatory, tailoring-friendly turn in bank oversight after the 2023 failures. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence she will ease capital/supervision burdens on regional and community banks.
persons.drivers: Community-bank advocacy; reducing regulatory burden; supervisory predictability.
persons.worldview: Light-touch Republican; skeptical of one-size-fits-all capital rules and of aggressive Basel 'endgame' increases.
persons.decision_style: Pragmatic, industry-grounded, incremental; dissents toward less stringent rules.
persons.track_record: Dissented against parts of the Basel III endgame and climate-supervision moves; elevated to the supervision role in 2025 to lead a deregulatory agenda.
persons.vulnerabilities: Her elevation reopens the post-SVB 'are we loosening too soon' debate; community-bank ties feed capture concerns.
persons.relationships: Successor to the Quarles supervisory posture; counter to Brainard/Tarullo.
persons.outlook: Her agenda will set bank-capital and crypto-bank supervision for the current cycle.

persons.name: Neel Kashkari
persons.role: President, Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis (2016–present); TARP Administrator (2008–2009)
persons.bluf: The Treasury official who ran TARP at 35, later a vocal Minneapolis Fed president oscillating between dove and hawk and a persistent 'too-big-to-fail' critic. Assess with MODERATE confidence he is the FOMC's most public communicator and a TBTF-reform advocate.
persons.drivers: Public engagement; ending too-big-to-fail; being heard.
persons.worldview: Pragmatic; flips between dovish (pre-2021) and hawkish (post-2021 inflation) as data shifts; structurally skeptical of mega-bank safety.
persons.decision_style: Outspoken, media-forward, willing to publish detailed plans (the 'Minneapolis Plan' for TBTF).
persons.track_record: Administered the $700B TARP bank bailouts; ran (unsuccessfully) for California governor; as Minneapolis Fed pres pushed the TBTF-ending capital plan and shifted hawkish in 2022.
persons.vulnerabilities: The Goldman/Treasury/PIMCO arc and TARP role tie him to the bailout-establishment narrative he now critiques.
persons.relationships: Paulson-era Treasury alumnus; rotating FOMC voter.
persons.outlook: A bellwether communicator whose shifts telegraph FOMC sentiment.

persons.name: Donald Kohn
persons.bluf: A 40-year Fed careerist who was Greenspan's closest policy confidant and Bernanke's vice chair through the 2008 crisis — institutional memory personified. Assess with HIGH confidence on his role as a continuity anchor across the Greenspan-Bernanke transition and crisis response.
persons.drivers: Institutional stewardship; monetary-policy continuity; financial-stability learning.
persons.worldview: Mainstream, consensus-oriented; evolved from Greenspan-era light-touch toward post-crisis macroprudential thinking.
persons.decision_style: Quiet, deeply experienced, consensus-building; the staff-to-Board bridge.
persons.track_record: Greenspan's longtime monetary-policy chief, then crisis-era vice chair; later served on the Bank of England's Financial Policy Committee.
persons.vulnerabilities: Associated with the Greenspan-era 'maestro' regime whose light touch preceded 2008.
persons.relationships: Greenspan and Bernanke's right hand; trans-Atlantic regulatory link via the BoE FPC.
persons.outlook: A continuity node linking the pre- and post-2008 Fed regimes.

persons.name: Roger Ferguson
persons.role: Fed Vice Chair (1999–2006); CEO of TIAA (2008–2021)
persons.bluf: The Fed vice chair who was the only governor in DC on 9/11 and directed the central bank's response to keep the payment system running, later running the $1T-plus TIAA. Assess with HIGH confidence on the 9/11 crisis-management role; his post-Fed corporate-board career links monetary and corporate elites.
persons.drivers: System resilience; institutional credibility; bridging public and private finance.
persons.worldview: Mainstream, stability-focused; pragmatic crisis manager.
persons.decision_style: Steady, understated, operationally decisive under stress.
persons.track_record: Led the Fed's 9/11 liquidity and payments response; chaired the BIS Committee on the Global Financial System; ran TIAA for 13 years; serves on major corporate boards (incl. Alphabet).
persons.vulnerabilities: Corporate-board portfolio invites revolving-door framing, though without specific controversy.
persons.relationships: Greenspan-era vice chair; connector to large-cap corporate governance (Alphabet) relevant to the AI-capital map.
persons.outlook: A bridge node between the Fed, large asset management (TIAA), and Big Tech governance.

persons.name: Hester Peirce
persons.role: Commissioner, SEC (2018–present); head of the SEC Crypto Task Force (2025–present)
persons.bluf: The SEC's leading crypto advocate — 'Crypto Mom' — who proposed a token 'Safe Harbor,' dissented repeatedly against the Gensler-era enforcement-first approach, and now leads the Crypto Task Force writing the rules. Assess with HIGH confidence she is the principal internal architect of the SEC's 2025 pro-crypto pivot.
persons.drivers: Regulatory clarity over enforcement; limited-government principles; rule-of-law process.
persons.worldview: Free-market, due-process-focused; views 'regulation by enforcement' as illegitimate and innovation-killing.
persons.decision_style: Principled dissenter, prolific writer of detailed dissents; now in a rule-writing leadership role.
persons.track_record: Proposed the Token Safe Harbor (2020/2021); dissented against most Gensler crypto enforcement actions; appointed to lead the 2025 Crypto Task Force shaping registration/custody/token guidance.
persons.vulnerabilities: Critics argue her stance is industry-aligned and risks under-protecting investors amid the very failures the project documents.
persons.relationships: Ideological ally of the Atkins-led SEC and of Waller's pro-stablecoin Fed posture; foil to Crenshaw and to Gensler-era enforcement.
persons.outlook: Her task force's output will define the US crypto regulatory perimeter — central to the SEC/SDNY and stablecoin threads.

persons.name: Mark Uyeda
persons.bluf: The career SEC staffer-turned-commissioner who served as acting chair in early 2025 and rapidly began unwinding Gensler-era crypto and ESG rules before Atkins's confirmation. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence he executed the transitional deregulatory pivot at the agency.
persons.drivers: Deregulatory restoration; cost-benefit discipline; institutional process.
persons.worldview: Market-oriented Republican; skeptical of expansive disclosure mandates (climate) and enforcement-led crypto policy.
persons.decision_style: Process-driven, institutionally fluent (decades of SEC staff experience), incremental.
persons.track_record: As acting chair, paused/withdrew climate-disclosure defense, launched the crypto-rules reset, and set up the Crypto Task Force with Peirce before handing off to Atkins.
persons.vulnerabilities: Speed of the rollback draws process and investor-protection criticism.
persons.relationships: Aligned with Peirce and Atkins; transitional foil to the Gensler agenda.
persons.outlook: Helped set the trajectory the Atkins SEC now executes.

persons.name: Caroline Crenshaw
persons.bluf: The SEC's lone remaining Democratic commissioner and its most outspoken internal crypto skeptic and investor-protection hawk, dissenting against the 2025 deregulatory and crypto-accommodation turn. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence she is the principal dissenting voice on the current Commission.
persons.drivers: Investor protection; market integrity; guarding against systemic crypto risk.
persons.worldview: Pro-regulation Democrat; views many crypto products as non-compliant securities posing investor harm.
persons.decision_style: Detailed, data-driven dissents; willing to be the sole 'no' vote.
persons.track_record: Dissented against Bitcoin/Ether ETF approvals and crypto-rule rollbacks; flagged stablecoin and conflict-of-interest risks; her renomination became politically contested.
persons.vulnerabilities: Politically isolated on a Republican-majority Commission; her warnings are the on-record counter-narrative.
persons.relationships: Foil to Peirce/Uyeda/Atkins; aligned in spirit with Gensler-era and Warren-style investor-protection positions.
persons.outlook: Her dissents are the documented internal-record counterweight to the crypto pivot the project tracks.

persons.name: Richard Breeden
persons.bluf: The SEC chair who handled the S&L-era enforcement wave and the Salomon Brothers Treasury-auction-rigging scandal (1991), establishing the modern template for forcing reform on a systemically important firm. Assess with HIGH confidence on his role setting precedents for enforcement against market-rigging.
persons.drivers: Market integrity; aggressive enforcement; reform of compromised institutions.
persons.worldview: Enforcement-forward Republican; believed in tough sanctions to deter manipulation.
persons.decision_style: Assertive, willing to impose leadership changes (e.g., the Buffett-led Salomon cleanup).
persons.track_record: Drove the Salomon Treasury-auction-manipulation resolution; strengthened enforcement post-S&L; later a corporate-monitor and activist investor (WorldCom monitor).
persons.vulnerabilities: Era predates current tools; mainly of historical-precedent relevance.
persons.relationships: Bush-41 appointee; the Salomon case is a template echoed in later market-plumbing manipulation cases.
persons.outlook: Historical precedent node for enforcement against auction/market rigging relevant to the plumbing thread.

persons.name: David Ruder
persons.bluf: The SEC chair during the October 1987 'Black Monday' crash, whose handling of the program-trading and circuit-breaker aftermath shaped modern market-structure safeguards. Assess with HIGH confidence on his role in the post-1987 market-structure reforms.
persons.drivers: Market stability; understanding the 1987 crash mechanics; reform.
persons.worldview: Academic securities-law scholar; measured, study-driven.
persons.decision_style: Deliberative, professorial; emphasized inter-agency coordination (the SEC-CFTC jurisdictional fault line surfaced in 1987).
persons.track_record: Led the SEC through Black Monday; the crash's program-trading findings fed circuit breakers and the SEC/CFTC coordination debate.
persons.vulnerabilities: Limited modern footprint; historical-precedent relevance.
persons.relationships: His era exposed the SEC-CFTC split that recurs in today's crypto-derivatives jurisdiction fights.
persons.outlook: Historical node for market-structure fragility and the SEC/CFTC turf question.

persons.name: Harold Williams
persons.bluf: The Carter-era SEC chair who emphasized corporate-governance reform — board independence and audit-committee standards — laying groundwork still referenced in disclosure and governance debates. Assess with MODERATE confidence on his lasting governance-reform influence.
persons.drivers: Corporate governance; board accountability; disclosure quality.
persons.worldview: Reform-minded; believed independent boards and audit committees were the front line of investor protection.
persons.decision_style: Principled, governance-focused, academic-leaning.
persons.track_record: Pushed audit-committee and board-independence norms; later led the Getty Trust.
persons.vulnerabilities: Pre-modern era; mainly governance-precedent relevance.
persons.relationships: His governance agenda foreshadows today's audit-committee and disclosure-integrity concerns (cf. the Wendy Gramm/Enron audit-committee failure).
persons.outlook: Historical governance-reform node.

persons.name: James Newsome
persons.bluf: The CFTC chair who oversaw early-2000s energy-derivatives markets in the post-CFMA, post-Enron period, then immediately became president of NYMEX — a clean revolving-door exemplar in the commodity-exchange space. Assess with HIGH confidence on the chronology.
persons.drivers: Market development; light-touch commodity oversight; industry alignment.
persons.worldview: Deregulatory commodities regulator; favored exchange self-regulation.
persons.decision_style: Industry-friendly, pragmatic, transition-minded.
persons.track_record: Chaired the CFTC through the Enron aftermath without major re-regulation, then ran NYMEX (the dominant energy exchange) within months of leaving.
persons.vulnerabilities: The CFTC-to-NYMEX move is a textbook revolving-door case alongside Wendy Gramm's.
persons.relationships: Successor to the Gramm-era CFTC posture; part of the commodity-exchange capture pattern.
persons.outlook: Historical revolving-door node relevant to commodity-market plumbing and oversight.

persons.name: Walter Lukken
persons.bluf: The acting CFTC chair through the 2008 commodity-price spike and financial crisis, who then became the long-running head of the global futures-industry lobby (FIA) — embodying the regulator-to-industry-association pipeline. Assess with HIGH confidence on the role sequence.
persons.drivers: Derivatives-market functioning; industry representation; clearing-system advocacy.
persons.worldview: Market-oriented; champions central clearing and the futures-industry model.
persons.decision_style: Technocratic, industry-fluent, relationship-driven.
persons.track_record: Led the CFTC during the 2008 oil spike and Lehman; later ran the FIA, lobbying on clearing, capital, and crypto-derivatives rules.
persons.vulnerabilities: The CFTC-to-FIA arc is a capture-narrative exhibit; FIA's positions track member-firm interests.
persons.relationships: Bridge between CFTC alumni and the clearing/derivatives industry relevant to the market-plumbing thread.
persons.outlook: Industry-lobby node connecting regulation and the clearing complex.

persons.name: Reuben Jeffery III
persons.role: Chair, CFTC (2005–2007); Under Secretary of State for Economic Affairs (2007–2009)
persons.bluf: The Goldman-Sachs-partner-turned-CFTC-chair-turned-State-Department-economic-chief who personifies the Goldman-to-government circuit running through commodity-market regulation. Assess with HIGH confidence on the career arc; influence interpreted.
persons.drivers: Public service from a Wall Street base; market-friendly regulation; economic diplomacy.
persons.worldview: Establishment Republican; market-oriented; internationally networked.
persons.decision_style: Polished, relationship-driven, transition-fluent across finance, regulation, and diplomacy.
persons.track_record: Goldman partner before chairing the CFTC, then Under Secretary of State for Economic Affairs, later CEO of Rockefeller & Co.
persons.vulnerabilities: The Goldman/CFTC/State/Rockefeller path is a dense revolving-door/network exhibit.
persons.relationships: Part of the Goldman-government network alongside Paulson, Bannon-era figures, and others in the roster.
persons.outlook: Network node linking Goldman, commodity regulation, and economic statecraft.

persons.name: Changpeng Zhao
persons.bluf: Founder of the world's largest crypto exchange, who pleaded guilty in 2023 to Bank Secrecy Act violations (failure to maintain an effective anti-money-laundering program) in a $4.3B DOJ/Treasury settlement and served four months. Assess with HIGH confidence on the guilty plea and settlement; his post-release re-emergence is a live variable.
persons.drivers: Market dominance; the 'CZ' personal brand; protecting Binance's global liquidity franchise.
persons.worldview: Crypto-maximalist, jurisdiction-arbitraging; long operated Binance without a fixed legal home ('Binance has no headquarters').
persons.decision_style: Fast, centralizing, founder-dominant; reportedly ran AML/compliance as secondary to growth.
persons.track_record: Built Binance into the dominant exchange; the 2023 plea and settlement is the largest crypto enforcement action to date; his November 2022 tweets helped trigger the FTX bank-run; remains BNB's largest holder and an active investor.
persons.vulnerabilities: The BSA conviction; persistent regulatory exposure across jurisdictions; concentration of BNB holdings.
persons.relationships: Rivalry-then-collapse dynamic with SBF/FTX; Binance's reach touches Tether, market-making, and the stablecoin-rail debate.
persons.outlook: His re-emergence and Binance's compliance trajectory are central to crypto-market-structure risk.

persons.name: Richard Teng
persons.bluf: The former regulator (Singapore MAS, Abu Dhabi Global Market) installed as Binance CEO after CZ's guilty plea to lead a compliance-forward reset. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence his mandate is regulatory normalization while CZ retains ownership influence.
persons.drivers: Restoring Binance's regulatory standing; institutional legitimacy; continuity of the franchise.
persons.worldview: Compliance-oriented technocrat; believes engagement with regulators is survival.
persons.decision_style: Process-driven, regulator-fluent, deliberately low-key versus CZ's founder style.
persons.track_record: Headed markets at SGX and was a senior MAS/ADGM regulator before joining Binance; now steering its post-settlement compliance buildout and licensing push.
persons.vulnerabilities: Operates under the shadow of CZ's ownership and the firm's legacy exposure; credibility tied to whether the reset is real.
persons.relationships: Reports into a CZ-owned structure; counterpart to global regulators; relevant to the Gulf (ADGM) capital nexus.
persons.outlook: His success or failure determines whether the largest exchange becomes a compliant pillar or a recurring risk.

persons.name: Caroline Ellison
persons.bluf: The Alameda Research CEO whose cooperating testimony was central to Sam Bankman-Fried's fraud conviction; she pleaded guilty to seven counts and was sentenced to two years. Assess with HIGH confidence on the plea, cooperation, and her account of FTX's misuse of customer funds.
persons.drivers: Originally SBF-aligned ('effective altruism'); later cooperation and accountability.
persons.worldview: Quant-trader rationalism overlaid with EA utilitarian framing; deferred heavily to SBF.
persons.decision_style: Analytical but, by her own testimony, conflicted and directed by SBF on key decisions.
persons.track_record: Ran Alameda as it borrowed FTX customer deposits; pleaded guilty in late 2022; her detailed 2023 testimony (the 'Alameda balance sheet,' the secret borrowing) anchored the prosecution; sentenced to 24 months.
persons.vulnerabilities: The fraud admissions; her credibility rests on cooperation rather than exoneration.
persons.relationships: Former romantic and professional partner of SBF; co-defendant-turned-witness alongside Wang and Singh.
persons.outlook: A primary documentary source for how the FTX/Alameda commingling worked.

persons.name: Gary Wang
persons.bluf: FTX's co-founder and chief technology officer, who wrote the code giving Alameda its near-unlimited 'allow_negative' credit line on FTX, pleaded guilty, and cooperated; he received no prison time. Assess with HIGH confidence on the technical mechanism he implemented and his cooperation.
persons.drivers: Engineering execution; loyalty to SBF; later cooperation.
persons.worldview: Builder-engineer; implemented business directives without independent governance.
persons.decision_style: Technically dominant, deferential to SBF on product/financial decisions.
persons.track_record: Built the exchange's core software and the special back-door privileges for Alameda; pleaded guilty to fraud; testified for the prosecution; sentenced to time served.
persons.vulnerabilities: Authorship of the back-door code is the technical heart of the fraud case.
persons.relationships: Co-founder with SBF; cooperating co-defendant with Ellison and Singh.
persons.outlook: The engineering witness establishing how the customer-fund diversion was coded.

persons.name: Nishad Singh
persons.bluf: FTX's head of engineering, who pleaded guilty to fraud and campaign-finance conspiracy, cooperated, and was sentenced to time served plus supervised release. Assess with HIGH confidence on the plea and his testimony about the shortfall and straw-donor scheme.
persons.drivers: Technical contribution; EA idealism; remorse and cooperation.
persons.worldview: Mission-driven engineer disillusioned upon discovering the customer-fund hole.
persons.decision_style: Conscientious but slow to act on red flags he later testified he saw.
persons.track_record: Helped run FTX engineering; was a conduit in the illegal political-donations scheme; pleaded guilty and testified; sentenced to time served.
persons.vulnerabilities: The straw-donor admissions tie FTX to the campaign-finance thread.
persons.relationships: Cooperating co-defendant alongside Ellison and Wang; reported to SBF.
persons.outlook: A witness linking FTX funds to political-influence spending.

persons.name: Ryan Salame
persons.bluf: The FTX executive who ran the Bahamas entity and was the firm's most visible Republican-side political donor, pleaded guilty to campaign-finance and unlicensed-money-transmitting conspiracies, and was sentenced to 7.5 years. Assess with HIGH confidence on the plea and the bipartisan donor scheme it exposed.
persons.drivers: Political influence-building; FTX's regulatory/relationship strategy; personal ventures.
persons.worldview: Operator/fixer; viewed political giving as strategic infrastructure.
persons.decision_style: Relationship-driven, deal-making, politically networked.
persons.track_record: Directed FTX's right-leaning political donations (paralleling SBF's left-leaning giving), ran the Bahamas hub; pleaded guilty and received one of the longest FTX sentences.
persons.vulnerabilities: The donor scheme is a core campaign-finance-compromise exhibit; the longest non-SBF sentence underscores culpability.
persons.relationships: FTX's political-spending counterpart to SBF; linked to the dual-track donation strategy.
persons.outlook: Key node connecting crypto-exchange money to both parties' political finance.

persons.name: Sam Trabucco
persons.bluf: Alameda's co-CEO who stepped back from day-to-day management months before the collapse, leaving Ellison in sole control; he was not charged. Assess with MODERATE confidence on his diminished role in the final period; his exact knowledge is not publicly adjudicated.
persons.drivers: Quant trading; lifestyle (publicly stepped back, bought a boat); low public profile post-collapse.
persons.worldview: Trader-quant; ambiguous public stance on the firm's failures.
persons.decision_style: Data-driven trading focus; receded from leadership before the crisis.
persons.track_record: Co-ran Alameda's trading; announced reduced involvement in August 2022; has largely avoided public comment and was not indicted.
persons.vulnerabilities: His pre-collapse exit and silence leave open questions; not a subject of charges.
persons.relationships: Alameda co-leader with Ellison under SBF's umbrella.
persons.outlook: A peripheral but unresolved figure in the Alameda timeline.

persons.name: Su Zhu
persons.bluf: Co-founder of the crypto hedge fund Three Arrows Capital, whose June 2022 implosion — driven by leveraged bets including Terra/LUNA and a GBTC/stETH carry — cascaded into Celsius, Voyager, and BlockFi. Assess with HIGH confidence on 3AC's role as a primary 2022-contagion vector; he was detained in Singapore in 2023.
persons.drivers: Leveraged conviction bets; the 'supercycle' thesis; reputation as a macro-crypto sage.
persons.worldview: Crypto-permabull; believed in a perpetual bull 'supercycle,' which justified extreme leverage.
persons.decision_style: High-conviction, highly leveraged, opaque to creditors; evaded liquidators post-collapse.
persons.track_record: Ran 3AC's multi-billion book; the fund defaulted on loans across the lending complex, triggering systemic contagion; liquidators pursued him; detained in Singapore for non-cooperation.
persons.vulnerabilities: The leverage, the 'supercycle' miscall, and evasion of liquidators define the reputational damage.
persons.relationships: Counterparty to Genesis/DCG (Silbert), Celsius (Mashinsky), Voyager (Ehrlich); borrowed across the lending web.
persons.outlook: The canonical example of how concentrated leverage transmits crypto contagion.

persons.name: Kyle Davies
persons.bluf: Su Zhu's 3AC co-founder, equally responsible for the leveraged book that collapsed in 2022 and for the post-failure evasion of creditors. Assess with HIGH confidence on shared responsibility; he claimed non-Singaporean status to complicate jurisdiction.
persons.drivers: Leveraged conviction; the supercycle thesis; avoiding liability.
persons.worldview: Crypto-permabull mirroring Zhu; dismissive of creditor claims post-collapse.
persons.decision_style: High-leverage, opaque, evasive with liquidators; later launched a claims-trading venture (OPNX) that itself faltered.
persons.track_record: Co-managed 3AC's collapse; resisted liquidators alongside Zhu; the failed OPNX 'bankruptcy claims' exchange compounded the reputational fallout.
persons.vulnerabilities: Evasion of creditors; the OPNX follow-on failure.
persons.relationships: Equal counterparty to the lending complex (Genesis, Celsius, Voyager); partnered with Zhu throughout.
persons.outlook: Second principal of the contagion-originating fund.

persons.name: Alex Mashinsky
persons.bluf: Founder of the crypto lender Celsius, convicted in 2025 of securities and commodities fraud and market manipulation (of the CEL token) and sentenced to 12 years, after Celsius's 2022 freeze and bankruptcy wiped out depositors. Assess with HIGH confidence on the conviction and the deposit-loss harm.
persons.drivers: Personal brand ('unbank yourself'); CEL token value; aggressive yield promises.
persons.worldview: Crypto-populist; marketed Celsius as a safe high-yield bank alternative while taking outsized risk.
persons.decision_style: Promotional, risk-taking, by the verdict deceptive about Celsius's finances and CEL trading.
persons.track_record: Built Celsius to ~$25B in assets on unsustainable yields; froze withdrawals June 2022; filed bankruptcy; convicted 2025 of fraud and CEL manipulation; sentenced to 12 years.
persons.vulnerabilities: The fraud and manipulation conviction; the depositor losses; the gap between marketing and reality.
persons.relationships: Borrowed to/from the 3AC-era lending web; emblematic of the 2022 CeFi-lender collapse cohort.
persons.outlook: A central case in the algorithmic/CeFi-yield-failure and manipulation thread.

persons.name: Stephen Ehrlich
persons.bluf: CEO of the crypto broker Voyager Digital, which collapsed in 2022 after its loans to 3AC defaulted; the CFTC and FTC pursued charges alleging he misrepresented Voyager as safe and FDIC-insured. Assess with HIGH confidence on the collapse and the regulatory allegations; litigation outcomes are partly pending/settled.
persons.drivers: Brokerage growth; the 'safe, insured' marketing; high-yield customer acquisition.
persons.worldview: Retail-crypto-brokerage builder; downplayed counterparty and insurance risk.
persons.decision_style: Growth-focused; relied on a concentrated, ultimately fatal 3AC exposure.
persons.track_record: Grew Voyager on retail deposits, lent heavily to 3AC, froze and went bankrupt in 2022; CFTC/FTC actions alleged deceptive 'FDIC-insured' claims (the FDIC issued a cease-and-desist on those representations).
persons.vulnerabilities: The false-insurance marketing is a direct tie to the FDIC-misrepresentation and depositor-harm themes.
persons.relationships: Major 3AC counterparty; part of the 2022 lender-collapse cluster with Celsius and BlockFi.

persons.vulnerabilities: Hawkishness contested amid euro-area fragility; the digital-euro push faces political resistance.

persons.outlook: A central figure in the euro's monetary path and the CBDC-vs-stablecoin contest.

persons.name: Nicolai Tangen
persons.role: CEO, Norges Bank Investment Management (Norway's sovereign wealth fund)
persons.bluf: CEO of the world's largest sovereign wealth fund (~$1.7T), one of the single biggest shareholders in the Magnificent Seven and a prominent public voice on AI-driven market concentration. Assess with HIGH confidence on the fund's scale and its outsized exposure to the AI-equity complex the project centers on.
persons.drivers: Long-horizon returns for Norway; responsible-investment standards; navigating AI-era concentration.
persons.worldview: Index-anchored universal owner; publicly candid about bubble risk and tech concentration.
persons.decision_style: Transparent, communicative (podcasts/interviews), governance-active as a large minority holder.
persons.track_record: Runs the fund's vast passive-plus-active book; among the largest holders of Apple, Microsoft, Nvidia, Alphabet, Amazon, Meta; votes on executive pay and governance across the AI core.
persons.vulnerabilities: As a universal owner, the fund is structurally long the very concentration risk Tangen warns about.
persons.relationships: Major shareholder in every Magnificent Seven name; counterpart to GIC/CIC and other sovereign allocators.
persons.outlook: A bellwether owner whose commentary and votes touch the entire AI-equity bubble thesis.

persons.name: Lim Chow Kiat
persons.bluf: CEO of GIC, one of the largest and most secretive sovereign investors and an active allocator into AI, tech, and private markets globally. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence on GIC's significant role in funding the AI-capital and private-credit complex.
persons.drivers: Preserving Singapore's reserves' long-run purchasing power; diversification; access to top managers.
persons.worldview: Long-horizon, risk-managed allocator; cautious public posture, active private-market dealmaker.
persons.decision_style: Disciplined, discreet, manager-relationship-driven; emphasizes downside resilience.
persons.track_record: Directs GIC's global multi-asset book across public equities, private equity, credit, real estate, and infrastructure, including AI/tech and data-center exposure.
persons.vulnerabilities: Opacity limits external assessment; large private-market exposure carries mark-to-market and liquidity risk.
persons.relationships: Co-investor alongside Gulf funds, NBIM, and CIC; a funder in the data-center/AI-infrastructure buildout.
persons.outlook: A major (if quiet) capital source for the AI-infrastructure and private-credit threads.

persons.name: Peng Chun
persons.bluf: Head of China's ~$1.3T sovereign wealth fund, navigating the politically fraught task of deploying Chinese state capital into global (especially US) tech and financial assets amid decoupling. Assess with MODERATE confidence on CIC's constrained but significant global footprint.
persons.drivers: Returns on China's reserves; strategic access to technology/assets; operating within geopolitical limits.
persons.worldview: State-aligned allocator; increasingly steered toward non-US and Belt-and-Road-adjacent assets as US scrutiny rises.
persons.decision_style: Cautious, politically constrained, pivoting toward alternatives and emerging markets.
persons.track_record: Runs CIC's global book; faces CFIUS and outbound/inbound investment screening that limits US tech access; rotating toward other regions.
persons.vulnerabilities: Geopolitical constraints; transparency limits; US investment-screening exposure.
persons.relationships: Counterpart and rival to Western and Gulf sovereign funds; tied to the China-tech and decoupling threads.
persons.outlook: A barometer of how decoupling reshapes sovereign capital flows into AI/tech.

persons.name: Gita Gopinath
persons.role: First Deputy Managing Director, IMF (2022–2025); returning to Harvard
persons.bluf: The IMF's former #2 and a leading international macroeconomist on dollar dominance, capital flows, and 'geoeconomic fragmentation,' shaping the global policy frame for the very fragmentation the project documents. Assess with HIGH confidence on her intellectual influence over IMF crisis and fragmentation analysis.
persons.drivers: Global financial stability; rigorous open-economy macro; warning on fragmentation costs.
persons.worldview: Mainstream-but-empirical; emphasizes the dollar's 'dominant currency' role and fragmentation risks.
persons.decision_style: Research-driven, data-first, influential in IMF surveillance and the WEO framing.
persons.track_record: Co-led the IMF through pandemic and inflation shocks; authored influential work on dominant-currency pricing and geoeconomic fragmentation; returned to academia in 2025.
persons.vulnerabilities: IMF prescriptions remain contested in the Global South; influence is analytical rather than executive.
persons.relationships: Successor-lineage to Fischer/Rogoff at the IMF; her fragmentation frame underpins the geopolitics threads.
persons.outlook: Provides the canonical analytical lens for dollar-rail and fragmentation dynamics.

persons.name: Roberto Campos Neto
persons.bluf: The Brazilian central banker who moved early and aggressively on rate hikes (well ahead of the Fed) and championed the Pix instant-payment system and a 'Drex' CBDC, making Brazil a real-world test of EM monetary autonomy and digital money. Assess with HIGH confidence on his early-hiker and payments-innovation record.
persons.drivers: Brazilian price stability; central-bank autonomy; payments modernization (Pix/Drex).
persons.worldview: Orthodox inflation-fighter; pro-innovation on digital payments; defended CB independence against political pressure.
persons.decision_style: Decisive, front-running global tightening; technically confident.
persons.track_record: Hiked Selic far ahead of DM central banks (2021); launched Pix (now ubiquitous in Brazil); advanced the Drex CBDC pilot; clashed with the Lula government over rates.
persons.vulnerabilities: Political friction over autonomy; EM carry/currency volatility exposure.
persons.relationships: An EM-central-bank reference point in the carry-trade and CBDC-vs-stablecoin debates.
persons.outlook: A template for EM monetary autonomy and instant-payment adoption relevant to the rails thread.

persons.name: Michele Bullock
persons.bluf: The first woman to lead the RBA, steering a commodity-exposed, China-linked economy through the late-cycle inflation fight — a key node in the AUD carry and commodity-currency dynamics. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence on her role in the carry-relevant AUD rate path.
persons.drivers: Australian price and financial stability; RBA credibility after governance review; managing China-commodity exposure.
persons.worldview: Pragmatic, data-dependent; balances housing-debt sensitivity against inflation.
persons.decision_style: Measured, reform-minded (post-review RBA restructuring), communication-focused.
persons.track_record: Led RBA rate decisions through the 2023–2025 cycle and the post-review institutional overhaul; manages an economy highly geared to Chinese commodity demand.
persons.vulnerabilities: Housing-debt sensitivity constrains policy; commodity/China exposure adds external risk.
persons.relationships: AUD is a core carry-trade and commodity currency; ties to the China-demand and metals threads.
persons.outlook: A relevant node in the global carry and commodity-currency channels.

persons.name: Tiff Macklem
persons.bluf: The Bank of Canada governor managing a rate-sensitive, housing-heavy economy tightly coupled to the US, and an early G7 mover on rate cuts — a useful contrast to the Fed's path. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence on his role as a leading-indicator DM central banker.
persons.drivers: Canadian price stability; managing household-debt fragility; G7 coordination.
persons.worldview: Mainstream inflation-targeter; attentive to housing and US spillovers.
persons.decision_style: Cautious, data-dependent; willing to diverge from the Fed (earlier cuts).
persons.track_record: Led the BoC through the pandemic, the 2022 tightening, and an early-2024 pivot to cuts ahead of the Fed; oversees CBDC research.
persons.vulnerabilities: Housing-debt sensitivity; tight coupling to US conditions limits independence.
persons.relationships: A DM-central-bank comparator to the Fed; relevant to the rate-path and carry threads.
persons.outlook: An early-mover signal for DM monetary divergence.

persons.name: Mohammed Al-Jadaan
persons.bluf: Saudi Arabia's finance minister, steering the fiscal side of Vision 2030 and the kingdom's vast AI/tech capital deployment alongside the PIF. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence on his role in directing Gulf sovereign capital relevant to the AI-funding thread.
persons.drivers: Vision 2030 diversification; fiscal sustainability amid oil-price swings; attracting and deploying global capital.
persons.worldview: Reform-minded technocrat; aligns fiscal policy with MBS's diversification and AI-investment agenda.
persons.decision_style: Pragmatic, market-engaging, balances mega-project spending against deficits.
persons.track_record: Manages the Saudi budget and debt issuance funding Vision 2030; coordinates with the PIF (Al-Rumayyan) on AI/tech and data-center investment.
persons.vulnerabilities: Oil-price dependence; mega-project execution and deficit risks.
persons.relationships: Works with Al-Rumayyan (PIF, present) and global tech/AI partners; central to the Gulf-sovereign-AI-capital thread.
persons.outlook: A key director of the Gulf capital flowing into the AI buildout.

persons.name: Rachel Reeves
persons.role: Chancellor of the Exchequer, United Kingdom (2024–present)
persons.bluf: The UK's first female Chancellor, managing British fiscal credibility in the long shadow of the 2022 gilt/LDI crisis and steering the City's competitiveness and crypto/fintech regulation. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence on her role over UK fiscal and financial-regulatory direction.
persons.drivers: UK fiscal credibility and growth; City of London competitiveness; market confidence post-2022.
persons.worldview: Centre-left fiscal-discipline advocate; pro-investment, pro-stability after the Truss episode.
persons.decision_style: Rules-based (fiscal rules), market-attentive, growth-and-stability framing.
persons.track_record: Sets UK budgets and gilt issuance; oversees FCA/PRA financial-regulation direction including digital assets and pensions/LDI reform.
persons.vulnerabilities: Gilt-market sensitivity (the LDI precedent); growth-versus-discipline tradeoffs.
persons.relationships: Counterpart to BoE (Bailey, present); the gilt/LDI episode ties to the carry and plumbing threads.
persons.outlook: Stewards a market whose 2022 crisis is a template in the leverage/plumbing analysis.

persons.name: Shaktikanta Das
persons.bluf: The long-serving RBI governor who steered India through the pandemic, championed the digital-rupee CBDC and UPI's global expansion, and took a notably restrictive line on private crypto. Assess with HIGH confidence on his CBDC/UPI and crypto-restrictive record.
persons.drivers: Indian price and financial stability; promoting UPI/digital-rupee; guarding against private-crypto risk.
persons.worldview: Stability-first, pro-public-digital-money, crypto-skeptic central banker.
persons.decision_style: Pragmatic, gradualist, coordination-heavy with the government.
persons.track_record: Led RBI through COVID, launched the e-rupee pilot, drove UPI's international tie-ups, and pushed restrictions on private crypto/stablecoins.
persons.vulnerabilities: Government-coordination raised independence questions; crypto stance pushed activity offshore.
persons.relationships: Predecessor to Malhotra; central to the India-swing-power and CBDC-vs-stablecoin threads.
persons.outlook: Architect of India's public-digital-money model now studied globally.

persons.name: Joachim Nagel
persons.bluf: The Bundesbank president and a leading ECB hawk, carrying Germany's stability-culture into euro-area monetary policy and the digital-euro debate. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence on his role anchoring the ECB's hawkish, stability-first wing.
persons.drivers: Euro-area price stability; German stability orthodoxy; credible normalization.
persons.worldview: Hawkish ordoliberal; wary of fiscal dominance and prolonged accommodation.
persons.decision_style: Disciplined, orthodox, vocal on inflation risks within the Governing Council.
persons.track_record: Backed the ECB's aggressive 2022–2023 hikes and balance-sheet reduction; supports a cautious digital-euro design.
persons.vulnerabilities: Hawkishness contested amid German industrial weakness and periphery fragility.
persons.relationships: Aligned with Schnabel/Knot on the hawkish axis; counterweight to dovish periphery voices.
persons.outlook: A core voice in euro-area rate-path and CBDC decisions relevant to the carry/rails threads.

persons.name: Katsunobu Kato
persons.bluf: Japan's finance minister overseeing the world's most indebted major sovereign and the yen-intervention authority — the fiscal counterpart to the BoJ as the yen carry trade unwinds. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence on his role over Japan's debt issuance and FX-intervention decisions.
persons.drivers: Japanese fiscal management; yen stability; financing the largest DM debt stock.
persons.worldview: Orthodox LDP fiscal stance; defends yen via intervention while debt constrains options.
persons.decision_style: Cautious, coordination-heavy with the BoJ and MOF currency officials.
persons.track_record: Directs JGB issuance and authorizes yen-intervention operations during episodes of sharp depreciation; manages debt-to-GDP near the global maximum.
persons.vulnerabilities: Extreme debt load; intervention efficacy limited against rate differentials.
persons.relationships: Fiscal counterpart to the BoJ (Ueda, present); the yen carry unwind is a core global-risk channel.
persons.outlook: Central to the yen-carry and DM-debt-sustainability threads.

persons.name: Sanjay Malhotra
persons.bluf: The current RBI governor and former revenue secretary, inheriting India's digital-rupee, UPI-globalization, and crypto-policy agenda as the country balances Western and BRICS ties. Assess with MODERATE confidence on his early direction continuing the stability-and-public-digital-money line.
persons.drivers: Indian monetary and financial stability; growth support; advancing digital public infrastructure.
persons.worldview: Technocratic continuity; expected to sustain CBDC/UPI promotion and crypto caution.
persons.decision_style: Administrative, fiscally fluent (ex-revenue secretary), pragmatic.
persons.track_record: Took office in late 2024; steers RBI policy on rates, the e-rupee, and digital-asset regulation amid India's swing-power positioning.
persons.vulnerabilities: Limited track record as governor; government-coordination/independence questions persist.
persons.relationships: Successor to Das; works alongside Sitharaman; tied to the India-swing-power thread.
persons.outlook: Will shape India's monetary and digital-money trajectory in the decoupling era.

persons.name: Michael Kratsios
persons.role: Director, White House Office of Science and Technology Policy (2025–present); Assistant to the President for Science & Technology
persons.bluf: The administration's top science-and-technology official and chief AI-policy coordinator, a Thiel-network alumnus (Thiel Capital, Scale AI) who also held the CTO role in the first Trump term. Assess with HIGH confidence on his central role steering federal AI strategy and his operator-network lineage.
persons.drivers: US technological supremacy (esp. AI); deregulation of emerging tech; the Thiel-network agenda.
persons.worldview: Techno-optimist, light-touch-regulation; sees AI/quantum leadership as a national-security imperative.
persons.decision_style: Policy-fluent, network-connected, executes a pro-industry acceleration agenda.
persons.track_record: First-term US CTO; later Scale AI managing director; now OSTP director coordinating the AI executive orders, compute, and R&D priorities.
persons.vulnerabilities: Industry ties (Scale AI, Thiel) feed conflict-of-interest and capture critiques given his AI-policy authority.
persons.relationships: Core node of the Thiel/operator-network in government; coordinates with Sacks (AI/crypto czar) and Krishnan.
persons.outlook: A principal architect of federal AI policy central to the project's AI-capital thesis.

persons.name: Bo Hines
persons.role: Former Executive Director, President's Council of Advisers on Digital Assets (2025)

persons.bluf: The inaugural executive director of the White House crypto council, the operational coordinator of the administration's pro-digital-asset agenda (the Bitcoin Reserve, stablecoin policy) under czar David Sacks, before departing to the private crypto sector. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence on his coordinating role in crypto policy.
persons.drivers: Advancing the US crypto agenda; the Strategic Bitcoin Reserve; industry-friendly rulemaking.
persons.worldview: Crypto-maximalist policy operator; views digital assets as strategic national infrastructure.
persons.decision_style: Fast-moving, industry-engaging, coordination-focused; left government for a crypto venture.

persons.vulnerabilities: Rapid government-to-industry exit underscores the revolving-door pattern; industry alignment.
persons.relationships: Worked under Sacks (crypto czar, present); tied to the GENIUS Act and Bitcoin-Reserve threads.
persons.outlook: A node connecting the White House crypto agenda to the stablecoin and reserve threads.

persons.name: Jacob Helberg
persons.role: Under Secretary of State for Economic Growth, Energy & the Environment (2025–present)
orgs: US-China Economic and Security Review Commission
persons.bluf: A China-tech hawk and former Palantir senior adviser elevated to a top State economic post, bridging Silicon Valley's national-security wing and US economic statecraft on chips, AI, and critical minerals. Assess with HIGH confidence on his operator-network lineage and China-decoupling agenda.
persons.drivers: Winning the US-China tech contest; allied techno-economic strategy; the 'techno-economic' national-security doctrine.
persons.worldview: Hawkish techno-nationalist; argues the US must wield economic and tech levers against China.
persons.decision_style: Networked, doctrine-driven (authored 'The Wires of War'), industry-and-security-aligned.
persons.track_record: Palantir adviser and USCC commissioner before confirmation; now drives State's economic-security agenda on export controls, minerals, and energy.
persons.vulnerabilities: Palantir ties and Silicon-Valley-security-network alignment feed capture/conflict critiques.
persons.relationships: Part of the Thiel/Palantir operator-network in government; aligned with Colby on China-first strategy.
persons.outlook: A node linking the operator-network, Palantir, and economic statecraft to the chip/minerals threads.

persons.name: Elbridge Colby
persons.role: Under Secretary of Defense for Policy (2025–present)
persons.bluf: The Pentagon's top policy official and the leading proponent of a 'China-first' defense strategy that prioritizes deterring a Taiwan conflict over other theaters — directly relevant to the silicon-shield and chip-war stakes. Assess with HIGH confidence on his role shaping US force-posture priorities toward China/Taiwan.
persons.drivers: Deterring China over Taiwan; prioritizing the Indo-Pacific; hard strategic tradeoffs.
persons.worldview: Realist restrainer-hawk; argues finite US resources must concentrate on the China threat.
persons.decision_style: Doctrine-driven (authored 'The Strategy of Denial'), willing to deprioritize Europe/Middle East.
persons.track_record: Drove the National Defense Strategy's China focus; advocates Taiwan-defense prioritization and allied burden-sharing.
persons.vulnerabilities: Prioritization stance is contested by Europe-first and Middle-East hawks; allied anxieties.
persons.relationships: Aligned with Helberg/Cotton on China; shapes the Taiwan-silicon-shield calculus.
persons.outlook: Central to the defense-strategy dimension of the chip-chokepoint and Taiwan threads.

persons.name: Stephen Feinberg
persons.role: Deputy Secretary of Defense (2025–present); co-founder, Cerberus Capital Management
persons.bluf: The Pentagon's #2, a billionaire private-equity founder (Cerberus) whose appointment fuses big private capital with the defense-industrial base he now helps run. Assess with HIGH confidence on the PE-to-Pentagon linkage; specific conflict management is interpreted.
persons.drivers: Defense-industrial efficiency; modernization; applying private-capital discipline to DoD.
persons.worldview: PE-operator pragmatist; views defense acquisition through an investor's cost/return lens.
persons.decision_style: Deal-driven, results-focused, low public profile.
persons.track_record: Built Cerberus (incl. defense-adjacent holdings like DynCorp historically); now Deputy SecDef overseeing budgets and the industrial base.
persons.vulnerabilities: Cerberus's financial-sector and defense holdings create conflict-of-interest exposure given his role.
persons.relationships: Links private capital (the project's 'Capital' layer) directly to the defense-industrial base.
persons.outlook: A node fusing PE capital and Pentagon procurement relevant to the defense and capital threads.

persons.name: Jared Isaacman
persons.role: Entrepreneur; private astronaut (Polaris/Inspiration4); former NASA Administrator nominee
persons.bluf: The payments billionaire (Shift4) and SpaceX-flown commercial astronaut whose NASA-leadership nomination tied the space agency tightly to the Musk/SpaceX orbit before a contentious withdrawal/renomination saga. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence on his SpaceX linkage and fintech base.
persons.drivers: Commercial spaceflight; payments-industry growth; public-private space leadership.
persons.worldview: Commercial-space accelerationist; close to the SpaceX commercial-human-spaceflight program.
persons.decision_style: Risk-taking entrepreneur-aviator; self-funded private missions.
persons.track_record: Founded Shift4 Payments; funded and commanded Inspiration4 and Polaris Dawn (SpaceX); nominated to lead NASA in a politically turbulent process.
persons.vulnerabilities: SpaceX dependence and payments-industry interests create conflict questions for any space-policy role.
persons.relationships: Tied to Musk/SpaceX (present); bridges fintech capital and the space-launch layer.
persons.outlook: A node connecting payments capital, SpaceX, and federal space policy.

persons.name: Brendan Carr
persons.bluf: The FCC chairman and a 'Project 2025' telecom author, driving spectrum, satellite (Starlink-relevant), national-security equipment bans, and a contested content/'censorship' agenda. Assess with HIGH confidence on his role over telecom, satellite, and the China-equipment security perimeter.
persons.drivers: US telecom/satellite leadership; countering China (Huawei/ZTE rip-and-replace); deregulation; content-policy reform.
persons.worldview: Deregulatory hawk; pro-satellite-broadband, anti-China-equipment, skeptical of Big Tech content moderation.
persons.decision_style: Aggressive, agenda-driven, willing to use FCC authority expansively.
persons.track_record: Authored the FCC chapter of Project 2025; advances satellite-broadband (favorable to Starlink), the Covered List equipment bans, and spectrum policy; pursues content/Section 230 actions.
persons.vulnerabilities: The Starlink-favorability perception and content-policy moves draw conflict and free-speech critiques.
persons.relationships: Policy benefits intersect Musk/Starlink; aligned with the China-decoupling agenda.
persons.outlook: A node over the satellite/telecom layer and the ad-censorship thread.

persons.name: Andrew Ferguson
persons.bluf: The FTC chairman steering antitrust and consumer-protection policy toward Big Tech and AI, continuing major platform cases while signaling a lighter merger-review touch elsewhere. Assess with HIGH confidence on his central role in Big-Tech antitrust and AI-competition policy.
persons.drivers: Reining in Big Tech market power; protecting children online; pro-business merger predictability.
persons.worldview: Conservative antitrust enforcer; targets platform dominance and content/censorship concerns while easing some merger scrutiny.
persons.decision_style: Litigation-continuing, ideologically selective, child-safety-focused.
persons.track_record: Inherited and pressed the Meta and Amazon antitrust cases; launched tech-censorship and child-safety inquiries; recalibrated merger guidelines.
persons.vulnerabilities: Selective enforcement invites inconsistency critiques; political-content focus is contested.
persons.relationships: Counterpart to DOJ Antitrust (Slater); central to the Meta/child-safety and ad-censorship threads.
persons.outlook: A node over Big-Tech antitrust relevant to the AI-platform and child-safety threads.

persons.name: Gail Slater
persons.role: Assistant Attorney General, DOJ Antitrust Division (2025–present)
persons.bluf: The DOJ antitrust chief and former Vance economic adviser, leading the government's landmark monopolization cases against Google and the broader Big-Tech/AI competition agenda. Assess with HIGH confidence on her role driving the Google remedies and tech-antitrust enforcement.
persons.drivers: Restoring competition in tech; the populist-right antitrust agenda; AI-market openness.
persons.worldview: New-right antitrust enforcer; skeptical of Big Tech and gatekeeper power.
persons.decision_style: Case-driven, remedy-focused (the Google search-monopoly remedies), tech-literate.
persons.track_record: Former IP/tech-policy lawyer and Vance aide; now runs the Google search and ad-tech monopolization remedy phases and AI-competition scrutiny.
persons.vulnerabilities: Aggressive remedies face appellate and market-structure risk; political-economy framing contested.
persons.relationships: Counterpart to FTC (Ferguson); tied to the Vance/operator-network orbit; central to the Google/Big-Tech threads.
persons.outlook: A node over the Google/Big-Tech monopolization cases relevant to the AI-platform thesis.

persons.name: Peter Navarro
persons.role: Senior Counselor to the President for Trade and Manufacturing (2025–present)
persons.bluf: The administration's combative trade-and-manufacturing counselor and intellectual driver of the tariff agenda and China-decoupling, returning to power after a contempt-of-Congress imprisonment. Assess with HIGH confidence on his role championing aggressive tariffs and reshoring.
persons.drivers: Reshoring US manufacturing; punitive tariffs on China; trade-deficit reduction.
persons.worldview: Hardline protectionist/economic-nationalist; views trade as a zero-sum contest with China.
persons.decision_style: Confrontational, ideologically rigid, willing to defy institutions.
persons.track_record: Architected first-term tariffs; served prison time for contempt of the Jan-6 committee; returned to drive the 2025 tariff escalation and supply-chain/critical-minerals policy.
persons.vulnerabilities: Tariff efficacy and inflation costs are contested; the contempt conviction; institutional friction.
persons.relationships: Aligned with the trade-hawk wing; tariffs interact with the chip-war, REE, and inflation threads.
persons.outlook: A primary driver of the tariff/decoupling dynamics rippling through the macro and chip threads.

persons.name: Robert Lighthizer
persons.role: Former US Trade Representative (2017–2021); influential trade-policy architect
persons.bluf: The architect of the first Trump-term tariff and USMCA strategy and the intellectual godfather of modern Republican protectionism, whose framework underpins the current trade-war approach. Assess with HIGH confidence on his foundational influence over the tariff doctrine, whether or not he holds a formal current post.
persons.drivers: Rebalancing trade; defeating China's mercantilism; reshoring industry.
persons.worldview: Strategic protectionist; treats tariffs as leverage and industrial policy.
persons.decision_style: Disciplined negotiator, legally rigorous (trade-law background), patient.
persons.track_record: Led the Section 301 China tariffs, USMCA, and the Phase One deal; authored 'No Trade Is Free'; his doctrine shapes current trade strategy.
persons.vulnerabilities: Tariff costs/efficacy contested; influence is doctrinal where not formally positioned.
persons.relationships: Intellectual lineage for Navarro/Greer; foundational to the trade-war thread.
persons.outlook: The doctrinal source of the tariff strategy driving chip/minerals/macro dynamics.

persons.name: Vivek Ramaswamy
persons.role: Entrepreneur; co-leader (initial), Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE)
persons.bluf: The biotech/asset-management entrepreneur and anti-ESG crusader who initially co-led the DOGE government-cutting initiative with Musk before pivoting to an Ohio gubernatorial run. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence on his role and anti-ESG capital-markets influence.
persons.drivers: Shrinking the administrative state; anti-ESG capitalism; political ascent.
persons.worldview: Libertarian-populist; opposes ESG/stakeholder capitalism and 'managerial' bureaucracy.
persons.decision_style: High-energy, media-driven, ideologically aggressive.
persons.track_record: Founded Roivant (biotech) and Strive (anti-ESG asset manager); briefly co-led DOGE; left to run for Ohio governor.
persons.vulnerabilities: Short DOGE tenure; Strive's anti-ESG positioning is itself a capital-markets stance under scrutiny.
persons.relationships: Briefly paired with Musk on DOGE; anti-ESG work touches the asset-management/capital layer.
persons.outlook: A node in the DOGE/anti-ESG capital-markets dynamics.

persons.name: Chris Wright
persons.role: Secretary of Energy (2025–present); founder & former CEO, Liberty Energy
persons.bluf: The fracking-executive-turned-Energy-Secretary driving an 'energy dominance' agenda of oil/gas expansion, grid buildout for AI data centers, and nuclear (incl. SMR) acceleration. Assess with HIGH confidence on his role over the power and HALEU/nuclear chokepoints central to the AI-energy thread.
persons.drivers: US energy dominance; powering AI/data-center demand; nuclear and LNG expansion.
persons.worldview: Fossil-and-nuclear maximalist; skeptical of climate-driven supply constraints; pro-abundance.
persons.decision_style: Industry-grounded, data-center-aware, deregulatory.
persons.track_record: Built Liberty Energy (fracking services); board ties to Oklo (SMRs); now leads DOE on grid expansion, the AI power gap, LNG approvals, and the HALEU/nuclear-fuel supply problem.
persons.vulnerabilities: Oil/nuclear-industry ties create conflict questions; climate-policy reversal is contested.
persons.relationships: Directly governs the power-adequacy and HALEU/Russia chokepoints the project formalizes.
persons.outlook: Central to the energy/power dimension of the AI-buildout thesis.

persons.name: Jonathan McKernan
persons.role: Treasury Under Secretary for Domestic Finance nominee (2025); former FDIC Board member
persons.bluf: A former FDIC board director tapped for senior Treasury/CFPB roles, positioned at the center of bank-regulation, debanking, and consumer-finance policy after the 2023 failures and the Chokepoint-2.0 debate.
